name: Time Rewind
description: Previously recorded state snapshots are replayed backward or restored to an earlier point under player or system control.
design_notes: Rewind is a state ownership problem before it is a visual effect. Each recorded_state_channel needs a sampling cadence, memory budget, restore order, and exclusion rule for systems such as achievements, network messages, random generation, UI, and irreversible narrative choices.
design_purpose: Let players recover from mistakes without fully restarting a challenge.
Create puzzles based on reversing object motion, player position, or causal sequences.
Expose experimentation by making recent consequences reversible while preserving chosen irreversible stakes.
player_fantasy: The player feels able to pull a mistake back through history and try a smarter line.

parameters.name: rewind_buffer_seconds
parameters.description: Recent history retained for possible restoration, constrained by memory and replay requirements.

parameters.name: rewind_sample_rate_hz
parameters.description: Snapshot sampling cadence for channels that cannot be reconstructed from deterministic input replay.

parameters.name: recorded_state_channel
parameters.description: State channels captured into the rewind buffer and restored in a defined order.

parameters.name: memory_budget_mb
parameters.description: Maximum memory allocation for history buffers before compression, decimation, or channel exclusion is required.

parameters.name: restore_collision_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule used when restoring actors into spaces that may now be occupied or invalid.

edge_cases: A rewind tries to restore an object that has been destroyed, pooled, unloaded, or replaced by a streamed chunk.
Random events, loot drops, or network messages occurred during the rewound interval and must not be duplicated.
The restored collision pose intersects moving platforms, doors, enemies, or newly spawned objects.
Inventory grants, quest flags, currency rewards, achievements, or irreversible narrative choices occur during the rewound interval and need exclusion or compensation rules.
Projectiles, status effects, summons, and enemy AI blackboard state must restore owner_id, lifetime, target_id, and last_known_position in a deterministic order.
Save, checkpoint, autosave, or scene streaming events occur while the player is actively rewinding.
Audio and camera rewind smoothly while authority gameplay snaps to a discrete snapshot.
common_bugs: Only transform state is recorded, so velocity, animation phase, health, cooldowns, and AI blackboard state resume incorrectly.
Snapshot buffers grow without bounds when multiple actors enter the recorded_state_channel set.
Restored physics bodies wake with stale contact pairs and launch the player after the first solver step.
Rewinding through save, checkpoint, or achievement events grants rewards more than once.
Projectiles are restored without source owner, team, or remaining lifetime and damage the wrong target after rewind.
Enemy AI resumes with a stale target_id or last_known_position that no longer matches restored world state.
The RNG stream is not restored, so loot, encounters, or procedural events diverge from the visible rewound history.
balancing_notes: Long buffers encourage experimentation but can remove tension unless objectives or resources define what remains irreversible.
Full-world rewind is expensive and powerful; single-channel rewind is easier to balance but must be communicated clearly.
Limit rewind in competitive or multiplayer contexts unless rollback authority and fairness rules are explicit.
accessibility_notes: Rewind can be a strong accessibility aid when it reduces repetitive failure loops; expose clear availability and remaining history.
Provide reduced motion and reduced audio-scrub options for players sensitive to reversed camera or sound effects.

implementation_notes.unity: Use ring buffers keyed by entity_id and recorded_state_channel rather than cloning whole GameObjects. Capture Rigidbody pose, velocity, angular velocity, Animator state hash, and gameplay fields at rewind_sample_rate_hz, then restore through a collision-aware transaction before re-enabling simulation.
implementation_notes.godot: Store compact snapshots for NodePath or stable entity ids in an autoload history service. PhysicsServer bodies need transform, linear velocity, angular velocity, sleeping state, and collision layer restoration, while excluded channels should be documented for deterministic replay.
implementation_notes.unreal: Record Actor state through components that implement a rewind snapshot interface, and keep network rollback windows separate from player-facing rewind. Chaos bodies need transform and velocity snapshots, while replicated games should let the server approve restore points.
implementation_notes.web: Use circular typed arrays or pooled objects for high-frequency channels to avoid garbage collection spikes. For Phaser, Matter.js, or custom simulation, restore position, velocity, active flags, and deterministic RNG stream ids together so replayed history matches captured history.

relationships.reason: Cooldown clocks must define whether rewind restores elapsed time, remaining duration, or consumed charges.
Rewind snapshots must include grapple attachment, rope length, velocity, and collision state to replay traversal safely.
Rewind restore order must handle platform position, carried passengers, contact state, and velocity transfer together.
Player-controlled rewind conflicts with ordinary online co-op authority unless rollback and shared timeline rules are designed first.

example_games.title: Braid
example_games.note: Uses rewind as a central puzzle and failure-recovery mechanic.

example_games.title: Prince of Persia: The Sands of Time
example_games.note: Uses rewind to recover from recent platforming and combat mistakes.
